text: The patient is positioned in the left lateral position with a kidney bridge for laparoscopic right adrenalectomy. Four ports are visible: two 10mm ports for the camera and instruments, a 12mm port for potential stapler use, and a 5mm port for liver retraction. The camera port is placed in the paramedian position, four fingerbreadths lateral to the obliques, with working ports on either side and a sublipoid port for liver retraction.

The surgeon begins dissection by releasing the liver attachments to expose the kidney and adrenal gland in the supracolic compartment. The liver is retracted using the subcephoid port, bringing the adrenal tumor into view. Peripheral attachments of the adrenal gland are divided using a hook cautery, with careful handling to minimize blood pressure surges. The liver attachments of the adrenal tumor and gland are further divided using the hook cautery, with no retraction or traction applied to the adrenal gland or tumor.

The surgeon begins medial dissection by excising the duodenum attachments, exposing the inferior vena cava (IVC) beneath. Using a hook cautery, flimsy adhesions between the adrenal gland and the IVC are carefully divided, avoiding injury to the IVC. Mild traction is applied to the adrenal gland in consultation with the anesthetist to prevent blood pressure surges. The adrenal vein is now visible and being dissected.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right adrenal vein, which is shorter and stockier. After dissection, the vein is secured with two clips on the patient's side and one clip on the specimen side before being divided.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects an accessory adrenal vein on the right side, clips it, and divides it. The adjacent adrenal artery is also clipped and divided using a ligature. Once the vessels are divided, the adrenal gland is lifted and freed, allowing for traction. Fibro-fatty tissues and lower attachments of the perinephric fat are divided using a ligature, ensuring no adrenal tissue is left behind. The dissection progresses from the medial to the lateral aspect, joining the previously completed lateral dissection.

The surgical field is irrigated with saline to identify and address any small bleeders or oozing, which are managed using electrocautery and ligature. A drain is placed through the lateral port. The adrenal specimen is placed in an endo bag, and the camera port is extended to facilitate specimen removal. The post-operative view shows the surgical site after completion.
